coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe2+/3+ by carbon dots doped lanthanide coordination polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yuanyuan
Zhang, Zhenzhen
Lv, Peiyao
Duan, Yinghao
Li, Gaiping
Ye, Baoxian
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we proposed a ratiomatric fluorescent strategy for sensing Fe2+ by using the carbon dots (CDs) doped lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln-CPs). Herein, the Ln-CPs were prepared by self-assemble of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) with Tb3+ (AMP-Tb) through coordination reaction, and CDs were encapsulated during the assembly process as guest materials. A cofactor, phenanthroline (Phen), could incorporate into the CDs@AMP-Tb, and strongly sensitized the luminescence of Tb3+ through energy transfer. Therefore, the obtained CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb could emit dual fluorescence signals. One is the characteristic emission of Tb3+, which could be specifically quenched by Fe2+ due to the high affinity between Phen and Fe2+. Another is originated from CDs encapsulated in Ln-CPs, which did not change in the presence of Fe2+. Subsequently, CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb was chosen as the ratiomatric fluorescent probe for sensing Fe2+, where the fluorescence of Tb3+ was quenched serving as the reporter, while that of CDs remained constant serving as the internal control for self-calibration. In addition, with the help of a reducing agent, which can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ efficiently, the proposed method can also be applied in selective detection Fe3+. By taking advantage of the unique property of Ln-CPs, the proposed strategy promises flexibility for designing of a series of ratiometric fluorescent sensors and expanded in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide coordination polymer
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Fe2+
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Switching on the luminescence of Tb3+ in CDs@AMP-Tb
Ratiomatric assay for Fe2+ and Fe3+
Determination of Fe2+/3+ in tap water
Synthesis and characterization of dual-emission probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb
The response of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb towards Fe2+ and the possible mechanism
The ratiometric fluorescence assay for Fe2+ detection
The ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe3+
The application of proposed sensor in determination of Fe2+/3+ in tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra (λex = 354 nm) of CDs, AMP-Tb, CDs@AMP-Tb, CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the mixtures prepared by separate addition of Phen, Fe(Phen)3, CDs@AMP-Tb, CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb, and CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb + Fe2+ in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4), respectively. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the mixture of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb + Fe2+, and its supernatant and the suspension of precipitate after centrifugation.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension with different concentrations of Fe2+ in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) upon illumination at 335 nm. (B) Plot of the ratios (I548- I442)/I442 versus the concentration of Fe2+ (Inset: calibration curve of the sensor at lower concentrations of Fe2+ from 2 to 500 nM).

Selectivity and competition experiments of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb towards different metal ions. Black columns represent the luminescence ratios (I548-I442)/I442 of sensing suspension after addition of metal ions (130 μM) as marked, and red columns represent that of in the presence of 100 μM Fe2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension with different concentrations of mixed solution (Fe3+ + NH2OH·HCl) in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) upon illumination at 335 nm. (B) Plot of the ratios (I548- I442)/I442 verse the concentration of Fe3+ (Inset: calibration curve of the sensor at lower concentrations of Fe3+ from 2 to 800 nM).
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of switching on and off the luminescence of CDs@AMP-Tb.
Determination of Fe2+/3+ in tap water.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe2+/3+ by carbon dots doped lanthanide coordination polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we proposed a ratiomatric fluorescent strategy for sensing Fe2+ by using the carbon dots (CDs) doped lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln-CPs). Herein, the Ln-CPs were prepared by self-assemble of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) with Tb3+ (AMP-Tb) through coordination reaction, and CDs were encapsulated during the assembly process as guest materials. A cofactor, phenanthroline (Phen), could incorporate into the CDs@AMP-Tb, and strongly sensitized the luminescence of Tb3+ through energy transfer. Therefore, the obtained CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb could emit dual fluorescence signals. One is the characteristic emission of Tb3+, which could be specifically quenched by Fe2+ due to the high affinity between Phen and Fe2+. Another is originated from CDs encapsulated in Ln-CPs, which did not change in the presence of Fe2+. Subsequently, CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb was chosen as the ratiomatric fluorescent probe for sensing Fe2+, where the fluorescence of Tb3+ was quenched serving as the reporter, while that of CDs remained constant serving as the internal control for self-calibration. In addition, with the help of a reducing agent, which can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ efficiently, the proposed method can also be applied in selective detection Fe3+. By taking advantage of the unique property of Ln-CPs, the proposed strategy promises flexibility for designing of a series of ratiometric fluorescent sensors and expanded in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In the human body, iron is the essential microelement and most abundant transition metal, which possesses crucial physiological functions in many biological processes. It is the third most metal existed in enzymes, and thus its biological functions are often considered to be involved as an essential cofactor in enzyme [1,2]. However, the trace amounts of free iron ions also exist under normal circumstances and play vital roles in oxidative stress, especially in the case of overload of iron storage or overproduction of super-oxides. Iron ion, presenting in both ferrous ion (Fe2+) and ferric ion (Fe3+), can interconvert between them through donating or accepting an electron. Both the abnormal level of iron and its disturbed redox balance may cause the disorder of cellular homeostasis and metabolism, and further lead to various diseases [3–6]. In this regard, detection of free iron ions with high selectivity over other metal ions is significant for human health. To date, many works have been reported to sense Fe3+ based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism due to its paramagnetic nature. However, there are few reports referring to selective fluorescent analysis of Fe2+ over Fe3+ [3,7,8].

Nowadays, lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln-CPs), have gained more and more attentions because of its unique optical properties, as well as structural tailorability, easy preparation, and adaptive ability to encapsulate guest materials [9–13]. Particularly, there have been a couple of ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on Ln-CPs by entrapping quantum dots or dye molecules as an internal calibration [14–19]. Compared with the traditional fluorescent turn-on or turn-off detection types, which is easily affected by the fluctuations associated with instrument and environment, these ratiometric fluorescent probes can provide accurate results by self-calibration of two emission bands, and thus makes it more desirable for the practical applications [14–21]. To our knowledge, the ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Fe2+ by using of carbon dots (CDs) dotted Ln-CP has not been achieved.

In this work, we aimed to construct a ratio fluorescent sensor for sensing Fe2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. We firstly prepared the CDs@AMP-Tb by self-assemble of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) with Tb3+ to construct the supramolecular networks, in which CDs were incorporated as guest nanomaterials through adaptive inclusion ability. When introducing phenanthroline (Phen), which could strongly sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+ through energy transfer, the obtained CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb displayed dual fluorescence signals [22–25]. One was the strong characteristic emissions of Tb3+ due to both the coordination networks bind with AMP and the efficient energy transfer from Phen. The other was originated from CDs encapsulated in the supramolecular networks of CPs. There were several advantages make this dual-emission fluorescent probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb particularly suitable for practical application. First, the higher specific and stronger binding ability between Phen and Fe2+ than other metal ions, could endow with a superior selectivity for Fe2+ assay. Second, the doped CDs, which were obtained by a facile method with stable fluorescence and good bio-safety [26,27], played as a non-interfering internal calibration made it possible to be a ratiometric sensor. Third, this fluorescent probe could also be applied for detecting Fe3+ with the help of a proper reducing agent, which could effectively reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+.

Adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and 1, 10-phenanthroline (Phen) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trisodium citrate dihydrate and urea were obtained from Tianjin Recovery Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tb(NO3)3·5H2O, 2-[4-(Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl] ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl), and other chemicals, such as amino acids and metal salts were purchased from Aladin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents involved were of analytical-grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was purified by a Thermo Scientific Barnstead GenPure water purification system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) with resistivity above 18.2 MΩ cm. HEPES buffer solution (0.1 M) was prepared by dissolving HEPES in ultrapure water, and the pH value was adjusted by NaOH solution (1.0 M). All the experiments were carried out at room temperature.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 500, Gemini, Japan) was used to characterized the morphologies. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a 970CRT fluorescence spectrometer using a xenon lamp as excitation source (Shanghai, China). The slit width was 5 nm for both excitation and emission. UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined by a Lambda 35 UV–Vis spectrometer (Pgeneral Instrument, Beijing, China).

CDs were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In general, trisodium citrate dehydrate (4.67 mmol) and urea (28 mmol) were dissolved in pure water (10 mL), and then the mixture was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 185 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, a faint yellow solution was obtained.

CDs@AMP-Tb was prepared by a self-assemble process as reported previously [14]. Firstly, the obtained CDs solution with different volumes was mixed well with HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4, 2 mL) containing AMP (10 mM). Then, Tb(NO3)3 aqueous solution (10 mM, 2 mL) was added and the mixture was stirred gently for 30 min at room temperature. The products were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 5 min), and washed with pure water for several times until no fluorescence of CDs in the supernatant was found. Finally, the precipitate was re-dispersed in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, 4 mL) to form CDs@AMP-Tb suspension, and stored at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Switching on the luminescence of Tb3+ in CDs@AMP-Tb
ce:section.ce:para: Phen was introduced as a cofactor ligand, which played as the sensitizer to switch on the luminescence of Tb3+ in CDs@AMP-Tb. Briefly, an aqueous solution of Phen (10 mM) was successively added into the CDs@AMP-Tb suspension (500 μL), and then the subsequent fluorescence spectra were monitored until the fluorescence intensity at 548 nm reached the maximum value. To investigate the effect of reaction time, the fluorescence spectra of system were recorded at every 3 min interval after the addition of Phen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiomatric assay for Fe2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In the case of Fe2+ assay, 60 μL of Phen solution (10 mM) was added into the CDs@AMP-Tb suspension (500 μL) firstly, and then the mixture was shaken well and stood by at room temperature for 15 min until the fluorescence intensity became stable. Subsequently, different volumes of Fe2+ stock solution (1 mM) were continuously added into the mixture and the fluorescent spectra were monitor ed accordingly.

For the indirect determination of Fe3+, a certain amount of NH2OH·HCl was added into Fe3+ solution in advance, and the resulting mixture was allowed to stand by for 30 min to finish the redox reaction. Later, different volumes of the reaction solution were continuously added into the CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension and the resulting fluorescence spectra were recorded. The effect of NH2OH·HCl on the fluorescence of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb was also investigated for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe2+/3+ in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Fe2+/3+ in tap water, different volumes of Fe2+ or Fe3+ stock solution (1 mM) were spiked into tap water respectively to prepare the samples. Then, six samples with different concentrations of Fe2+ or Fe3+ (pretreated with NH2OH·HCl for 30 min in advance) were added into the CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension (500 μL). Then, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under constant illumination with 335 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of dual-emission probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb
ce:section.ce:para: As an ideal bio-molecular ligand, AMP processed large coordination numbers and high coordination flexibility, and could coordinate with lanthanide ions to form the amorphous coordination polymers (CPs) through self-assembly process [28]. Meanwhile, in the course of self-assembly, some guest materials such as dyes, nanoparticles, and proteins could be encapsulated into the supramolecular networks of CPs due to their adaptive inclusion properties [29,30]. In our work, we firstly prepared the CDs through hydrothermal method. The CDs solution emitted blue fluorescence with one peak located at 442 nm under 354 nm irradiation ( Fig. 1, black curve). Then, CDs@AMP-Tb was synthesized by self-assemble of AMP with Tb3+ in aqueous solution at room temperature in the presence of CDs. Because of the weak f-f transition absorption of Tb3+, as well as the quenching effect of water molecules occupying the residual electron orbitals, the AMP-Tb was non-luminescence [30–32] (Fig. 1, red curve). When CDs were introduced, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs@AMP-Tb showed a strong emission peak centered at 442 nm, which is same to that of CDs, indicating the successful incorporation of CDs in the CPs. Meanwhile, a new small sharp peak locating at 548 nm appeared, which is attributed to one of the typical emission of Tb3+ (Fig. 1, green curve). The weak luminescence of Tb3+ might be due to the energy transfer from CDs upon excitation [33]. The ability of AMP-Tb to enwrap CDs was studied and the amount of CDs encapsulated in the CPs was optimized (Fig. S1). SEM images showed that AMP-Tb presented a nanoparticle aggregation structure with average size of about 30 nm, and the incorporation of CDs did not cause obvious changes in the morphology (Fig. S2A, B, Supporting information).

Generally, it is a conventional way to sensitize the luminescence of lanthanide complexes by introducing of an appropriate cofactor ligand, which replaced the coordinating water molecules and coordinated with lanthanide ions. Under irradiation, it can strongly absorb light energy and transfer energy to lanthanide ions, and thus significantly enhances the luminescence of lanthanide ions. This kind of sensitization process is called antenna effect, and the cofactor ligand is call antenna ligand [9,34]. Herein, we choose Phen as the antenna ligand. As expected, when we added Phen into the CDs@AMP-Tb suspension, three strong emission peaks centered at 491, 548, and 589 nm belonging to the characteristic emission of Tb3+ appeared (Fig. 1, blue curve). This could be due to the antenna effect of Phen [8,24], which displaced some coordinating water molecules around Tb3+, and then transfer energy to Tb3+ under irradiation. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Phen was investigated (Fig. S3, Supporting information), and the optimal Phen concentration was set to be 1.2 mM when the fluorescence intensity reached the highest value. Subsequently, the excitation wavelength was optimized to be 335 nm by comparing the fluorescent signal both of CDs and Tb3+ in CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb (Fig. S4, Supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb towards Fe2+ and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Upon exposure of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb to Fe2+, the typical emission of Tb3+ decreased obviously, while that of CDs was retained unchanged. It is well known that Phen, which was often used as a specific reagent for spectrophotometric determining of trace Fe2+, has stronger binding ability with Fe2+ than other common metal ions to form a stable complex [35,36]. Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that, Phen molecules might be excluded from the Tb3+ coordination sphere to form complex Fe(Phen)3, and thus its antenna effect was largely weakened, leading to the inefficient energy transfer and remarkable fluorescence quenching of Tb3+.

To validate this, we investigated the optical property of related materials by UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2A, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs@AMP-Tb dispersed in aqueous solution showed an obvious absorption peak at 260 nm, which is attributed to the characteristic adsorption of nucleobases in AMP [37,38]. When Phen was added, a strong absorption peak at 265 nm was found, which is same to that of pure Phen, implying that the adsorption of Phen played a stronger role in antenna effect as a cofactor ligand to sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+ than AMP [8]. Upon further addition of Fe2+, the absorption peak in the ultraviolet range at 265 nm blue shifted to 260 nm, accompany with the emerge of a new broad absorption in the range of 360–580 nm corresponding to the characteristic absorption of Fe(Phen)3 complex. These results indicated that the formation of Fe(Phen)3 complex. Besides the UV–vis spectroscopy measurement, a faint orange color could be observed by the naked eye after addition of Fe2+. After centrifugation, the orange color supernatant liquid displayed the characterization broad absorption of Fe(Phen)3 (Fig. 2B), further indicating that Phen was excluded from CPs and coordinated with Fe2+ to form a stable water-soluble Fe(Phen)3 complex.

Hence, the possible mechanism for Fe2+ assay by using the fluorescent probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb was schematically shown in Scheme 1. Briefly, CDs@AMP-Tb constructed from self-assemble of AMP and Tb3+ through coordination reaction, in which CDs were encapsulated during the assembly process as guest materials. The formed CDs@AMP-Tb only emitted CDs fluorescence because of the non-luminescence of AMP-Tb. Phen could replace water molecules and coordinate with Tb3+, and then effectively sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+ through the antenna effect. Upon addition of Fe2+, as evidenced by the above absorption spectra, it could snatch Phen from CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb to form a stable Fe(Phen)3 complex, owing to its superior affinity toward Phen to Tb3+ [8,39,40]. Therefore, the antenna effect of Phen was remarkably suppressed, and subsequently the fluorescence of Tb3+ was quenched significantly. Meanwhile, in the process of switching off the luminescence of Tb3+ by Fe2+, the fluorescence of CDs encapsulated in CPs almost did not change. Therefore, it is reasonable to develop a new ratiometric strategy for specific sensing of Fe2+ based on the fluorescent probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence assay for Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: A more quantitative analysis of Fe2+ was made by analyzing the response signals defined as the ratios (I548 - I442)/I442 (I548 and I442 are the fluorescence intensities at 548 nm for Tb3+ and that at 442 nm for CDs). upon addition of Fe2+. Specifically, the fluorescent intensity associated with Tb3+ decreased gradually when the concentration of Fe2+ was increased from 2 nM to 130 μM, while that at 442 nm originated from CDs encapsulated in the Tb-AMP did not change even at high concentrations of Fe2+ ( Fig. 3A). The concentration of Fe2+ could be quantitative by the ratio (I548 - I442)/I442. As shown in Fig. 3B, there were two linear relationship between the response signals and Fe2+ concentration: one is in the range of 2–500 nM (R2 = 0.994), and the other is 500 nM-130 μM (R2 = 0.995). The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 2 nM (S/N = 3).

We also investigated the response of the proposed sensor towards other common metal ions to test its selectivity. As expected, except for Fe2+, there was no significant change in the ratios upon addition of other metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ ( Fig. 4 black column). In addition, another set of competition experiment was also performed in the presence of Fe2+. The ratios were almost the same as that when only Fe2+ was added (Fig. 4 red column), concluding that these metal ions as referred had negligible interferences for Fe2+ detection. It could be concluded that the proposed sensor revealed superior selectivity in determination of Fe2+, and the high selectivity over other metal ions presumably owed to the strong specific and high binding affinity between Fe2+ and Phen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that Fe3+ could be facilely reduced to Fe2+ by a reducing agent, it might be viable to detect Fe3+ by using the proposed sensor with the aid of a reducing agent. Herein, we chose NH2OH·HCl as the reducing agent, which can substantially reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ [8]. As expected, when Fe3+ was pretreated with NH2OH·HCl for 30 min and added into the detection system, an obvious fluorescence quenching of Tb3+ was found. A series of control experiments confirmed that, either Fe3+ or NH2OH·HCl had no effect on the fluorescence signal of the CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension. This clearly indicated that Fe3+ was successfully reduced to Fe2+, and thus led to the decrease of the Tb3+ fluorescence. The quantitative assay of Fe3+ was carried out as described in the experimental section. Compared to Fe2+ assay, a similar phenomenon was achieved. As seen from Fig. 5, upon increasing the concentration of Fe3+, the fluorescent intensity of Tb3+ decreased gradually, while that of CDs was kept constant. The linear ranges of (I548- I442)/I442 with respect to Fe3+ concentration were 2–800 nM (R2 = 0.989) and 800 nM-130 μM (R2 = 0.995), with an LOD of 2 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of proposed sensor in determination of Fe2+/3+ in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, we further quantified Fe2+/3+ in tap water to investigate the practicality of the proposed sensor. Because the fluorescent signal of CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb suspension was remained virtually unchanged when tap water was added, which might be due to that the concentrations of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in tap water were lower than the LOD of our method, the standard addition method was employed herein. As can be seen from Table 1, the results were satisfying with recovery from 96.3% to 106.4% for Fe2+ and from 90.2% to 108.6% for Fe3+ in tap water respectively, indicating the ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed in this work could offer a reliable approach to simultaneously detect Fe2+ and/or Fe3+ in real sample such as tap water.

In summary, a ratiomatric fluorescent sensor for Fe2+ assay was successfully fabricated by using the dual emission probe CDs@AMP/Phen-Tb. The efficient sensitization effect of Phen, and its superior specific coordination ability with Fe2+ to other metal ions, endowed the sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity. The encapsulated CDs played as the internal control for self-calibration, which overcame the problems occurred in single change in fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, this sensor could also be applied in selective detection Fe3+ with the help of a reducing agent. The proposed strategy provided a generalizable platform for fabricating ratiometric fluorescent sensors by virtue of the unique features of Ln-CP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21575130 and 21305129) and Key Scientific Research Project in Universities of Henan Province (18A150017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.001.
